records.prompt: Write a migration guide for our engineering team: upgrading a production app from Next.js 15 to the current Next.js as of mid-2026. Cover the current version status, runtime requirements, breaking changes, and new build tooling defaults. Be specific about versions and API names.

baseline.report:
# Migration guide: upgrading a production app from Next.js 15 to current Next.js as of mid-2026

## Executive summary

As of mid-2026, the current stable Next.js line is **16.2.x**, and the official docs page showed **Latest Version: 16.2.9** in June 2026. Next.js **16.3** was in **preview**, not yet stable, in late June 2026.  
Sources:  
- Next.js version 16 upgrade docs: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16  
- Next.js blog index / release timeline: https://nextjs.org/blog  
- Next.js 16.3 preview posts: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations , https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-turbopack

For a production app on **Next.js 15**, the practical target in mid-2026 is **Next.js 16.2.x**, not 16.3 preview.

The biggest migration themes are:

1. **Runtime floor increases in 16**
   - **Node.js >= 20.9.0**
   - **TypeScript >= 5.1.0**
   - Browser baseline rises to **Chrome 111+, Edge 111+, Firefox 111+, Safari 16.4+**  
   Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

2. **Build tooling defaults change materially**
   - **Turbopack becomes the default bundler** in Next.js 16 for both dev and production builds for new/default workflows.
   - If you have a **custom `webpack` config**, `next build` can fail unless you explicitly opt back to webpack or migrate config.  
   Sources:  
   - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
   - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

3. **A set of deprecated 15.x behaviors are removed in 16**
   - `next lint` command removed
   - AMP removed
   - `experimental.dynamicIO` renamed/replaced by `cacheComponents`
   - `experimental.ppr` and `export const experimental_ppr` removed
   - sync access patterns for request APIs removed in 16
   - `middleware.ts` deprecated in favor of `proxy.ts` for the Node.js network boundary model  
   Sources:  
   - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5  
   - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
   - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

---

## Recommended upgrade path

For a production system, do this in two controlled steps:

### Phase 1: get clean on latest 15.x first
Upgrade to **Next.js 15.5.x** and eliminate all 16 deprecation warnings before jumping majors. Next.js 15.5 specifically introduced:
- `next lint` deprecation warnings
- deprecation warnings for upcoming Next.js 16 removals
- `next build --turbopack` beta
- stable Node.js runtime support for middleware in 15.x  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5

Why this matters: 15.5 is effectively the “prepare for 16” release.

### Phase 2: upgrade 15.5 -> 16.2.x
Once warnings are gone, upgrade to **16.2.x** and then validate:
- runtime compatibility
- bundler behavior under Turbopack
- request API async migrations
- image config changes
- routing/layout requirements

---

## Current version status and what to target

### Stable target
Target **Next.js 16.2.x** in mid-2026. The official docs showed **16.2.9** as latest in June 2026.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Do not target for production yet
**Next.js 16.3** was still a **preview** in June 2026. Its posts repeatedly describe it as “16.3 Preview” / “almost here.”  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-turbopack  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-ai-improvements

### Patch-level guidance
Because Next.js published multiple security notices affecting 15.x and 16.x users, you should use the **latest 16.2 patch** available, not just 16.0.  
Source timeline: https://nextjs.org/blog

---

## Runtime and platform requirements

## 1) Node.js
Next.js 16 raises the minimum Node.js version to **20.9.0** and drops Node 18 support.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Action**
- Upgrade CI, build images, Docker base images, and production runtimes to **Node 20.9+**
- Audit any platform-specific runtime constraints before the framework upgrade

## 2) TypeScript
Next.js 16 raises the minimum TypeScript version to **5.1.0**.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Action**
- Upgrade `typescript` to `^5.1.0` or later
- If using React typings, also update `@types/react` and `@types/react-dom`; the v15 guide explicitly calls this out during React 19 migration  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

## 3) React
Next.js 15 already moved the baseline to **React 19** / `react-dom` 19.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

**Action**
- Before jumping to 16, ensure the app is already stable on React 19 APIs
- Replace `useFormState` with `useActionState` where practical; React 19 keeps `useFormState` only as deprecated  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

## 4) Browser support
Next.js 16 browser baseline is:
- Chrome 111+
- Edge 111+
- Firefox 111+
- Safari 16.4+  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Action**
- Reconfirm customer/browser support commitments
- Re-run browser regression tests, especially if you still support older Safari variants

---

## Build tooling defaults: the biggest operational change

## 1) Turbopack is now the default bundler
Next.js 16 makes **Turbopack** the default bundler. The release notes say Turbopack reached stability for development and production builds and is the default bundler for new projects.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Operational implication**
If your app assumes webpack-specific behavior, the upgrade may fail or behave differently even if application code itself compiles.

### If you have a custom webpack config
The version-16 upgrade docs explicitly warn:

> If your project has a custom `webpack` configuration and you run `next build` (which now uses Turbopack by default), the build will fail...  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

**Action**
- Search `next.config.*` for a `webpack(config)` function
- If present, choose one of these paths:

#### Option A: short-term safety
Force webpack during migration:
- `next dev --webpack`
- `next build --webpack`  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

This is the safest first production rollout path.

#### Option B: migrate to Turbopack config
Move webpack-specific assumptions to supported Turbopack config where possible:
- top-level `turbopack`
- `turbopack.ignoreIssue`
- related Turbopack config APIs  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Babel behavior changes under Turbopack
In 16, **Turbopack automatically enables Babel if it finds a Babel config**, instead of hard-failing.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Action**
- If you have `.babelrc` / `babel.config.*`, test whether Babel transforms are still intended
- Measure build performance; unexpected Babel activation can slow builds

---

## 2) Turbopack filesystem caching defaults changed after 16.0

### In 16.0
Filesystem cache existed, but as beta / separately flagged behavior.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

### In 16.1
Turbopack filesystem caching for **`next dev`** became **stable and on by default**.  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopackFileSystemCache

The config docs record:
- `v16.1.0`: enabled by default for development
- build filesystem cache still experimental  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopackFileSystemCache

**Action**
- Expect changed local-dev performance characteristics after upgrading to 16.1+
- If debugging build-cache issues, inspect `.next` caching behavior

### In 16.2
No default change to build cache was documented as stable; build cache remained something you configure/experiment with.  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopackFileSystemCache

### In 16.3 preview
Persistent filesystem cache for **`next build`** appears in preview guidance, but 16.3 was not yet stable in mid-2026.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-turbopack

---

## 3) Linting defaults changed

### `next lint` is deprecated in 15.5 and removed in 16
15.5 introduced deprecation warnings; 16 removes the `next lint` command.  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

The 16 release notes say:
- use **ESLint directly** or **Biome**
- `next build` no longer runs linting
- codemod available:  
  `npx @next/codemod@canary next-lint-to-eslint-cli .`  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Action**
- Replace scripts like:
  - `"lint": "next lint"`
- With direct CLI scripts, e.g.:
  - `"lint": "eslint ."`
  - `"lint:fix": "eslint . --fix"`
- Move lint execution into CI explicitly

### ESLint config format default changes
`@next/eslint-plugin-next` defaults to **ESLint Flat Config** in 16.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Action**
- If still on legacy `.eslintrc*`, decide whether to:
  - keep legacy config temporarily, or
  - migrate to `eslint.config.mjs`

---

## Breaking changes and removals you must handle

## 1) Async Request APIs: 15 introduced it, 16 finishes the break

The v15 upgrade guide marks these request-time APIs as asynchronous:
- `cookies()`
- `headers()`
- `draftMode()`
- `params` in App Router file conventions
- `searchParams` in `page.js`  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

In v15, temporary synchronous access remained available with warnings/casts. In v16, the 16 release notes list sync access as removed behavior:
- must use `await cookies()`
- must use `await headers()`
- must use `await draftMode()`
- must use async `params` / `searchParams`  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Action**
Audit for all of the following patterns:

### Before
```ts
const c = cookies()
const h = headers()
const d = draftMode()
const { slug } = params
const q = searchParams.query
```

### After
```ts
const c = await cookies()
const h = await headers()
const d = await draftMode()

const { slug } = await params
const sp = await searchParams
const q = sp.query
```

In client components receiving promised props, use `use()` from React if needed, as documented in the v15 guide.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

---

## 2) `middleware.ts` -> `proxy.ts`
Next.js 16 introduces **`proxy.ts`** as the explicit Node.js network boundary file and says it replaces `middleware.ts` conceptually for that role. The release notes say:
- `proxy.ts` runs on the Node.js runtime
- `middleware.ts` still exists for Edge runtime use cases
- `middleware.ts` is deprecated and will be removed in a future version  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Action**
- If your middleware logic is Node-runtime-friendly and not specifically Edge-bound, plan to rename and adapt it to **`proxy.ts`**
- If you intentionally depend on Edge behavior, keep `middleware.ts` for now but treat it as technical debt

**Important nuance**  
In 15.5, **Node.js middleware runtime** became stable inside `middleware.ts` via `runtime: 'nodejs'`. In 16, the framework direction shifts toward **`proxy.ts`** for this boundary.  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

---

## 3) Caching model changes and API renames/removals

### `experimental.dynamicIO` -> `cacheComponents`
Next.js 16 removes `experimental.dynamicIO` and uses top-level **`cacheComponents`** instead.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

### `experimental.ppr` removed
The older PPR flags/config are removed:
- `experimental.ppr`
- route-level `export const experimental_ppr`  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

### New caching APIs in 16
Next.js 16 adds and/or formalizes:
- `updateTag()`
- `refresh()`
- `'use cache'`
- `cacheComponents`  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

### `revalidateTag()` signature change
In 16, `revalidateTag()` now expects a second argument for cache-life profile / SWR behavior:
```ts
revalidateTag('blog-posts', 'max')
```
Single-argument form is deprecated.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Action**
Search for:
```ts
revalidateTag('...')
```
and migrate to one of:
```ts
revalidateTag(tag, 'max')
revalidateTag(tag, 'hours')
revalidateTag(tag, 'days')
revalidateTag(tag, { expire: 3600 })
```
or use `updateTag(tag)` in Server Actions when you need read-your-writes behavior.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

---

## 4) Fetch and Route Handler caching semantics
Next.js 15 already changed defaults:
- `fetch()` requests are **no longer cached by default**
- `GET` Route Handlers are **no longer cached by default**  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

If your production app upgraded within the 15 line without fully auditing behavior, verify again before 16 because the new cache model (`cacheComponents`, `'use cache'`, `updateTag`, `refresh`) may prompt additional cleanup.

**Action**
Audit for endpoints/pages that silently relied on old implicit caching and choose explicit behavior:
- `cache: 'force-cache'`
- `export const fetchCache = 'default-cache'`
- route config such as `export const dynamic = 'force-static'`
- or explicit 16 cache components model  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

---

## 5) `next/image` security and default behavior changes

Next.js 16 changes multiple image defaults and restrictions:

- local `src` with query strings now requires **`images.localPatterns`**
- `images.qualities` default changes from `[1..100]` to `[75]`
- `quality` gets coerced to nearest allowed configured value
- `images.minimumCacheTTL` default changes from 60s to 14400s
- `images.imageSizes` drops `16`
- `images.maximumRedirects` default becomes `3`
- `images.dangerouslyAllowLocalIP` becomes a more explicit opt-in security exception
- `images.domains` is deprecated in favor of `images.remotePatterns`  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

15.5 specifically warned ahead of time about:
- `images.qualities`
- `images.localPatterns` for local image query strings  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5

**Action**
Audit all usages of:
- `<Image quality={...} />`
- local image URLs with query params like `/photo.jpg?v=1`
- `images.domains`

### Example migration
```ts
const nextConfig = {
  images: {
    qualities: [75, 100],
    localPatterns: [
      { pathname: '/photo.jpg', search: '?v=1' },
      { pathname: '/assets/**', search: '' },
    ],
    remotePatterns: [
      {
        protocol: 'https',
        hostname: 'images.example.com',
      },
    ],
  },
}
```
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

---

## 6) Parallel routes now require explicit `default.js`
In Next.js 16:
- all parallel route slots require explicit **`default.js`**
- builds fail without them
- previous implicit behavior must be replaced with `default.js` returning `null` or calling `notFound()`  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

**Action**
Search for `@slot` parallel routes in `app/` and ensure every slot has `default.js` / `default.tsx`.

---

## 7) `next/font` package cleanup
The v15 guide states the old `@next/font` package was removed in favor of built-in `next/font`.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

**Action**
If any older modules remain:
```ts
import { Inter } from '@next/font/google'
```
change to:
```ts
import { Inter } from 'next/font/google'
```

---

## 8) Other removals in 16 to check

From the 16 release notes, remove or replace:

- **AMP support**
  - remove `useAmp`
  - remove `export const config = { amp: true }`
  - remove `amp` config in `next.config.*`  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

- **`serverRuntimeConfig` / `publicRuntimeConfig`**
  - replace with environment variables / `.env*`  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

- **`experimental.turbopack` location**
  - move to top-level `turbopack`  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

- **Automatic smooth-scroll behavior**
  - if you depended on old automatic behavior, add `data-scroll-behavior="smooth"` to the HTML document  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

- **`unstable_rootParams()`**
  - removed pending future alternative  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

- **Metadata image route async params**
  - image-generating functions now receive promised `params`
  - `id` from `generateImageMetadata` becomes `Promise<string>`  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

---

## New defaults and stable features worth knowing after upgrade

These are not necessarily blockers, but they affect operational expectations.

## 1) Adapters API status
- In Next.js 16.0, Build Adapters API was introduced as alpha in release notes
- By Next.js 16.2, adapters were announced as **stable**
- `adapterPath` docs were updated in March 2026 and document the built-in adapters API  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/adapterPath

If you deploy to a platform with custom Next integration, confirm whether they now rely on `adapterPath`/adapters.

## 2) React Compiler support
Next.js 16 made built-in React Compiler support stable via the top-level `reactCompiler` option, but **it is not enabled by default**.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

This is optional; do not bundle it into the major upgrade unless you want an extra performance/compiler change at the same time.

## 3) Separate output directories / locking
Next.js 16 changed:
- `next dev` and `next build` use separate output directories
- lockfile mechanism prevents multiple instances of the same command on one project  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

This can affect custom tooling/scripts that inspect `.next` while builds/dev are running.

---

## Suggested migration checklist for the engineering team

## Pre-upgrade
- [ ] Upgrade to latest **15.5.x**
- [ ] Fix all deprecation warnings from 15.5
- [ ] Upgrade infra/runtime to **Node 20.9+**
- [ ] Upgrade `typescript` to **5.1+**
- [ ] Confirm app is already stable on **React 19**
- [ ] Replace `next lint` scripts with direct ESLint/Biome CLI
- [ ] Inventory any custom `webpack` config
- [ ] Inventory `middleware.ts`, AMP, `serverRuntimeConfig`, `publicRuntimeConfig`
- [ ] Inventory `next/image` custom quality / local query-string usage
- [ ] Inventory parallel routes needing `default.js`

## Code migration
- [ ] Convert all sync request APIs to async:
  - `await cookies()`
  - `await headers()`
  - `await draftMode()`
  - `await params`
  - `await searchParams`
- [ ] Replace old cache flags:
  - `experimental.dynamicIO` -> `cacheComponents`
  - remove `experimental.ppr`
  - remove `experimental_ppr`
- [ ] Update `revalidateTag(tag)` calls to `revalidateTag(tag, profile)`
- [ ] Rename/plan migration from `middleware.ts` to `proxy.ts` where appropriate
- [ ] Add explicit `default.js` to all parallel routes
- [ ] Replace `images.domains` with `images.remotePatterns`
- [ ] Add `images.localPatterns` if local image query params exist
- [ ] Configure `images.qualities` if non-75 quality values are used

## Build/test migration
- [ ] First run 16.2 builds with `--webpack` if custom webpack exists
- [ ] Validate Turbopack separately before making it default in CI/prod
- [ ] Re-run snapshot/visual tests for CSS ordering regressions
- [ ] Re-run route-level cache behavior tests
- [ ] Re-run browser support matrix against Safari 16.4+
- [ ] Move linting explicitly into CI because `next build` no longer does it

---

## Recommended rollout strategy for production

### Conservative rollout
If your production app has any nontrivial webpack customization, use this sequence:

1. Upgrade codebase to **16.2.x**
2. Keep builds on webpack temporarily:
   ```bash
   next build --webpack
   ```
3. Ship production
4. In a follow-up effort, validate Turbopack in CI/canary environments
5. Switch default production builds to Turbopack only after parity is proven

Why: official docs warn that custom webpack + default Turbopack can fail builds.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Aggressive rollout
If you have **no custom webpack config** and are already clean on 15.5 warnings:
1. Upgrade directly to latest 16.2.x
2. Run default Turbopack dev/build
3. Validate:
   - images
   - request API async changes
   - route slot defaults
   - lint pipeline
4. Deploy behind normal canary controls

---

## What not to include in the same change

To reduce blast radius, avoid combining the framework migration with:
- enabling `reactCompiler`
- adopting `cacheComponents` broadly as a product-level rearchitecture
- adopting 16.3 preview features like `partialPrefetching`
- changing deployment platform/adapters simultaneously unless required

These are valuable, but they are separate change streams.

---

## Final recommendation

For a production app in mid-2026, upgrade from **Next.js 15 -> 15.5 -> 16.2.x**, standardize on **Node 20.9+**, remove all deprecated 15-era APIs, migrate linting off `next lint`, and treat **Turbopack defaulting in 16** as the main operational risk—especially if you have any custom webpack configuration.

Short direct answer: **Target Next.js 16.2.x (latest stable was 16.2.9 in mid-2026), require Node 20.9+ and TypeScript 5.1+, migrate off deprecated 15 APIs, and plan carefully for Turbopack becoming the default bundler in 16.**

# Migration guide: upgrading a production app from Next.js 15 to current Next.js (mid-2026)

## Executive summary

As of mid-2026, the current stable Next.js line is **16.x**, with **16.2.10** listed as latest on 2026-07-01 by the widely used release tracker at endoflife.date, and the official Next.js site shows the active major release family as **Next.js 16** with official release notes for 16.0, 16.1, and 16.2.  
Sources:  
- https://endoflife.date/nextjs  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2  
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases

For a team upgrading a production app from **Next.js 15** to **Next.js 16**, the most important migration themes are:

1. **Build tooling default changed**: `next build` now uses **Turbopack by default** in Next.js 16. If your app has a custom `webpack` config, builds can fail until you migrate that customization or explicitly account for it.  
   Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

2. **Several previously deprecated items are now removed**:
   - `next lint` command removed in 16
   - AMP removed in 16
   - `middleware.ts` convention renamed to **`proxy.ts`**
   Sources:  
   - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5  
   - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16  
   - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy

3. **Async Request APIs and related signatures changed**:
   - Request-bound values such as `params` / `searchParams` moved toward async/promise-based handling in the upgrade docs
   - In 16, image generation functions also receive `params` and `id` as promises
   Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

4. **Caching model evolved further**:
   - Next 15 already changed defaults so `fetch`, `GET` Route Handlers, and client navigations are no longer cached by default
   - Next 16 pushes the newer caching model further with **Cache Components**, `use cache`, `cacheLife`, `updateTag()`, and a refined `revalidateTag()`
   Sources:  
   - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15  
   - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
   - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents  
   - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag

The safest production path is:

- First get your app to the latest **15.5.x** patch
- Clear all **15.5 deprecation warnings**
- Then upgrade to **16.x**
- Validate **Turbopack production builds**, **proxy migration**, **lint pipeline changes**, and **async/caching behavior** before rollout

---

## 1) Current version status as of mid-2026

### Recommended target
Target the latest stable **Next.js 16.2.x** patch available to your org at rollout time. As of 2026-07-01, endoflife.date lists **16.2.10** as latest.  
Source: https://endoflife.date/nextjs

### Why not stay on 15?
Next.js 15 is no longer the current line. It is listed separately from 16 on the support timeline, and 16 is the active major with current release notes and ongoing feature work.  
Sources:  
- https://endoflife.date/nextjs  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-1  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2

### Operational recommendation
For production, pin to a specific patch in the 16.2 line during rollout, not `latest`, and upgrade patch-by-patch after validation. The Next.js GitHub releases page is the authoritative patch history.  
Source: https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases

---

## 2) What changed between Next.js 15 and 16 that matters most

## 2.1 Build tooling default: Turbopack is now the default for builds

In Next.js 15.5, production Turbopack builds were still **beta** behind `next build --turbopack`.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5

In Next.js 16, **Turbopack is stable and the default bundler for all apps**. The official 16 release post calls this out directly, and the version 16 upgrade guide says `next build` now uses Turbopack by default.  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Practical impact
If your project defines a custom `webpack` function in `next.config.js` / `next.config.ts`, the Next.js 16 upgrade guide says **the build will fail** when `next build` runs, to prevent silent misconfiguration under Turbopack.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### What to do
Audit `next.config.*` for:
- `webpack(config, context) { ... }`
- loaders/plugins that only exist for webpack
- aliases/polyfills implemented only in webpack
- assumptions about emitted chunk layout

Then either:
- migrate equivalent settings to **`turbopack`** config where supported, or
- remove obsolete webpack customization, or
- if you genuinely depend on custom webpack behavior, treat this as a migration blocker and test thoroughly before production cutover

Relevant config references:  
- Turbopack config: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack  
- Webpack config: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/webpack

---

## 2.2 `next lint` is removed in Next.js 16

Next.js 15.5 introduced a deprecation warning: **`next lint` would be removed in Next.js 16**.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5

By the 16 upgrade guide, that removal is now part of the migration surface.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Practical impact
If your CI/CD scripts run:
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "lint": "next lint"
  }
}
```
you must replace that with your own ESLint invocation, e.g.:
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "lint": "eslint ."
  }
}
```

### Migration action
- Update package scripts
- Ensure ESLint is explicitly configured at repo level
- Make CI use `eslint` directly instead of Next’s removed wrapper

---

## 2.3 AMP is removed in Next.js 16

Next.js 15.5 explicitly warned that **AMP support would be removed in Next.js 16**.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5

### Practical impact
If your codebase still uses AMP-era APIs or page config, treat that as unsupported on 16 and remove it before or during the upgrade.

### Migration action
Search for:
- `amp: true`
- AMP-specific page config
- AMP-only rendering paths

If present, remove or replace them before cutover.

---

## 2.4 `middleware.ts` is renamed to `proxy.ts`

The Next.js 16 upgrade docs and API docs move this feature under the **`proxy`** file convention.  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy

The `proxy` docs include a **How to Migrate** section and clarify that the file convention is now `proxy.js` / `proxy.ts`.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy

### Practical impact
If your app currently uses:
- `middleware.ts`
- middleware matchers
- request rewriting/auth logic in middleware

you must validate the rename and confirm behavior under the new `proxy` convention.

### Additional caveat
The `proxy` docs note that the `runtime` config option is **not available in Proxy files**.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy

That matters if your current middleware depended on runtime configuration assumptions.

---

## 2.5 Async Request APIs and promise-based params

Next.js 15 already introduced significant migration around async request APIs and React 19-era patterns. The official upgrade guide shows examples where route params become promise-based and are awaited or unwrapped with React’s `use()`.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

Next.js 16 continues this direction. The version 16 upgrade guide explicitly notes that, to align with the **Async Request APIs** change, image-generating functions now receive **`params`** and **`id`** as promises.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Practical impact
Audit these APIs:
- `page.js` / `page.tsx` props
- `layout.js` / `layout.tsx` props
- `generateMetadata`
- image generation functions / Open Graph image handlers

If your code assumes synchronous `params` or `searchParams`, update signatures and reads to match the versioned docs.

### Example migration pattern from the official 15 guide
The 15 guide shows moving from:
```ts
type Params = { slug: string }
```
to:
```ts
type Params = Promise<{ slug: string }>
```
and then:
```ts
const { slug } = await params
```
or using:
```ts
import { use } from 'react'
const { slug } = use(params)
```
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

Do not bulk-apply this blindly; apply it where your code actually matches the affected API surfaces documented for your router mode and file type.

---

## 2.6 Caching behavior changed in 15, and 16 adds new caching APIs

### What changed in 15
The Next.js 15 release made caching semantics a breaking change:
- `fetch` requests are no longer cached by default
- `GET` Route Handlers are no longer cached by default
- client router navigations are no longer cached by default  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15

If your production app was upgraded to 15 without fully auditing behavior, you may already have latent cache regressions or hidden correctness fixes.

### What changes further in 16
Next.js 16 introduced **Cache Components** and newer cache APIs:
- `cacheComponents` config
- `use cache`
- `cacheLife`
- `updateTag()`
- changed guidance for `revalidateTag()`  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag

The `cacheComponents` docs say this flag enables component- and function-level caching using `use cache`, and explicitly tell upgraders from `experimental.useCache`, `experimental.dynamicIO`, or experimental PPR in 15 to use the version 16 guide.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents

The `revalidateTag` docs state that calling `revalidateTag` **without** the second argument is now deprecated; the recommended stale-while-revalidate pattern is:
```ts
revalidateTag('tag', 'max')
```
or a custom `cacheLife` profile, while `updateTag()` is the alternative when you want immediate consistency behavior.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag

### Practical impact
Audit:
- custom ISR/revalidation logic
- tags used with `revalidateTag`
- any experimental `dynamicIO`, `useCache`, or PPR flags
- assumptions that navigations or route handlers are cached by default

---

## 3) Runtime and platform requirements

## 3.1 React version requirements

The official Next.js 15 upgrade guide says the **minimum versions of `react` and `react-dom` are now 19**.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

The Next.js 16 line continues on React 19; public release coverage for the current line notes React 19.2 in the 16.2 generation, and the official release notes position 16 as the current React-19-era release family.  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2  
- https://github.com/vercel/next.js/releases

### Migration action
In `package.json`, verify alignment across:
- `next`
- `react`
- `react-dom`

Do not upgrade `next` alone.

---

## 3.2 Node.js/runtime considerations

The official upgrade pages surfaced in search do not clearly expose a single canonical “minimum Node version” line in the snippets returned here, so I am not going to invent one. What is clear from official 15.x and 16.x material is:

- Node.js runtime support for middleware became stable in **15.5**
- 16.2 adds `--inspect` support for `next start`
- 16.3 preview material references native Node.js streams improvements  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations

### Recommendation
Before rollout, confirm your exact Node.js version against:
- the `engines` field in the target `next` package you plan to install
- your hosting provider’s supported Node versions
- your lockfile/package manager behavior in CI

I could not confirm a precise official minimum Node.js version from the sources surfaced here, so your team should validate that directly in the target release metadata before production rollout.

---

## 3.3 Deployment platform / adapter implications

If you deploy outside Vercel or rely on a custom build/runtime integration, Next.js 16.2 introduced a **stable Adapter API** exposed via **`adapterPath`**.  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/nextjs-across-platforms  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/adapterPath

This may matter if:
- you maintain custom deployment glue
- you use OpenNext / Netlify / Cloudflare / Amplify style platform adapters
- you need consistent support for PPR/streaming/build outputs across environments

---

## 4) New defaults and config changes your team should audit

## 4.1 `next build` default behavior
**Before**: on 15.5, Turbopack builds were beta and opt-in with `--turbopack`  
**After**: on 16, `next build` uses Turbopack by default  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

## 4.2 CSS toolchain implications
The official config docs say:
- webpack uses PostCSS with `postcss-preset-env` by default unless Lightning CSS is enabled experimentally
- Turbopack uses **Lightning CSS by default**
- Turbopack always uses Lightning CSS  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/useLightningcss

### Practical impact
If your CSS behavior depends on webpack/PostCSS-specific transforms, browser target assumptions, or custom plugins, compare output under Turbopack carefully.

---

## 4.3 Caching config names
Use top-level:
- `cacheComponents`

and the related APIs:
- `cacheLife`
- `use cache`
- `updateTag`
- `revalidateTag(tag, profile)`  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag

If you used experimental flags from 15, the docs explicitly tell you to migrate through the 16 guide.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents

---

## 4.4 Server package bundling config
`serverExternalPackages` is the stable config name; the docs note it was renamed from `serverComponentsExternalPackages` and stabilized in v15.0.0.  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/serverExternalPackages

### Practical impact
If your Next 15 app still carries older experimental naming or assumptions, normalize to the stable API name before/while moving to 16.

---

## 5) Recommended production migration plan

## Phase 0: Inventory
Create a short audit list for the repo:

- `next.config.*`
- `middleware.ts`
- lint scripts using `next lint`
- AMP usage
- custom webpack loaders/plugins
- custom caching / `revalidateTag`
- experimental flags: PPR / dynamicIO / useCache
- route/layout/page props using `params` / `searchParams`
- OG/image generation handlers
- platform-specific deployment adapter code

## Phase 1: Stabilize on latest 15.5 patch
Upgrade first to the latest **15.5.x** patch and remove all deprecation warnings in CI.  
Why:
- 15.5 is the bridge release that warns about 16 removals
- it exposes deprecations for `next lint`, AMP, and upcoming 16 changes  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5

## Phase 2: Run the official codemod/upgrade path
Next.js officially recommends the `@next/codemod` upgrade flow.  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

Use the versioned codemod flow recommended by Next.js rather than hand-editing everything first.

## Phase 3: Migrate removed/renamed APIs
Mandatory items:
- replace `next lint`
- remove AMP
- migrate `middleware.ts` to `proxy.ts`

Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy

This is usually the biggest production risk.

Checklist:
- remove or migrate custom `webpack` config
- validate CSS output under Lightning CSS/Turbopack
- compare bundle size / chunking / sourcemaps
- verify third-party packages that depended on webpack-specific behavior

## Phase 5: Validate runtime semantics
Test:
- route caching
- `fetch` behavior
- GET route handlers
- auth redirects/rewrites in proxy
- RSC rendering and hydration
- image/metadata routes
- server actions / server functions logging and tracing where relevant

Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy

## Phase 6: Canary rollout
For production rollout:
- ship 16 behind a small traffic slice

- validate any CDN/proxy interactions with long React prerender headers if relevant  
Related config doc: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/reactMaxHeadersLength

---

## 6) High-risk areas to test explicitly

### A. Custom webpack config
Risk: build failure or changed behavior because 16 defaults to Turbopack  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### B. Auth / rewrites / headers logic in middleware
Risk: behavior drift when migrating to `proxy.ts`  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy

### C. Caching and stale data
Risk: unexpected uncached behavior from 15 defaults, plus changed tag revalidation semantics in 16  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag

### D. CSS output
Risk: Turbopack uses Lightning CSS by default, which may not match webpack/PostCSS behavior exactly  
Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/useLightningcss

### E. Async params/searchParams and metadata/image handlers
Risk: runtime errors or type errors from synchronous assumptions  
Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### F. CI lint jobs
Risk: broken CI after `next lint` removal  
Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5

---

## 7) Suggested code/config checks

## `package.json`
Look for and change:
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "lint": "next lint"
  }
}
```
to something ESLint-native, e.g.:
```json
{
  "scripts": {
    "lint": "eslint ."
  }
}
```
Source for removal/deprecation: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5

## `next.config.ts`
Audit for:
- `webpack`
- experimental cache/PPR flags
- old config names
- platform adapter needs
- `cacheComponents`

Relevant docs:  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/cacheComponents  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/adapterPath  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/serverExternalPackages

## File conventions
Search for:
- `middleware.ts` / `middleware.js`
- AMP configs
- `revalidateTag(` without a second argument
- synchronous `params` assumptions in App Router files

Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

---

## 8) Recommended target-state checklist

Before production cutover to Next.js 16, your team should be able to answer “yes” to all of these:

- We are on the latest validated **15.5.x** before jumping to 16  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5
- We removed all usage of **`next lint`**  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5
- We removed all **AMP** usage  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15-5
- We migrated **`middleware.ts` → `proxy.ts`** and retested auth/rewrites  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/file-conventions/proxy
- We validated **Turbopack** builds in CI and production-like environments  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- We audited **custom webpack** config and either removed or replaced it  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- We reviewed all **async request API** call sites  
  Sources:  
  - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15  
  - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- We reviewed caching logic for **`fetch`**, **GET Route Handlers**, **client navigations**, and **tag revalidation**  
  Sources:  
  - https://nextjs.org/blog/next-15  
  - https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/functions/revalidateTag
- We aligned `next`, `react`, and `react-dom` versions  
  Source: https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-15

---

## 9) Final recommendation

For a production app on Next.js 15, the best upgrade path in mid-2026 is to move to **Next.js 16.2.x**, but only after using **15.5.x as the staging point** to clear deprecations. The highest-risk change is not a UI API; it is the **build pipeline change to Turbopack-by-default** in 16, especially if your app has **custom webpack** configuration. The next highest-risk areas are **proxy/middleware migration**, **lint pipeline removal**, and **caching/async request API semantics**.

**Short direct answer:** Upgrade from 15 to the latest 15.5 patch first, remove `next lint`, AMP, and `middleware.ts` usage, then move to **Next.js 16.2.x** and validate **Turbopack-by-default**, async request APIs, and caching changes before production rollout.

It discusses async request APIs and warns to update synchronous assumptions, but does not clearly state that synchronous access to cookies(), headers(), params is fully removed in 16 and async-only.

# Briefing: 2026 FIFA World Cup state of play as of 4 July 2026

## Executive summary

The 2026 FIFA World Cup is in the **Round of 16** as of today, **4 July 2026**. This edition is the first with **48 teams**, split into **12 groups of four**. The top two teams in each group, plus the **eight best third-placed teams**, advanced to a new **Round of 32**, followed by the Round of 16, quarter-finals, semi-finals and final. FIFA says the tournament runs from **11 June to 19 July 2026** across **16 host cities** in the United States, Mexico and Canada.  
Sources: FIFA tournament overview and schedule:  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/match-schedule-fixtures-results-teams-stadiums  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

The Round of 32 has finished. The standout knockout shocks so far are **Paraguay eliminating Germany on penalties** and **Morocco knocking out the Netherlands on penalties**. There were also several tight escapes for contenders, including **Belgium coming from 2-0 down to beat Senegal 3-2 after extra time**, and **Argentina needing extra time to edge Cabo Verde 3-2**.  
Sources:  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/brazil-paraguay-round-32-review-highlights  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/netherlands-morocco-review-highlights  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/belgium-senegal-review-highlights  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/argentina-cabo-verde-match-report-highlights

## Tournament format

This World Cup is the largest in men’s tournament history:
- **48 teams**
- **12 groups of four**
- **Top two in each group** plus **eight best third-placed teams** advance
- Knockout bracket begins with a **Round of 32**
- Tournament dates: **11 June–19 July 2026**  
Sources:  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/match-schedule-fixtures-results-teams-stadiums  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

That format means the knockout phase now has four rounds before the final: **Round of 32, Round of 16, quarter-finals, semi-finals**, then the **final**.  
Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

## How the knockout stage has unfolded so far

### Completed Round-of-32 results

**29 June**

  Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/brazil-japan-review-highlights
- **Paraguay 1-1 Germany, Paraguay won 4-3 on penalties** — one of the tournament’s biggest upsets, confirmed by FIFA in its round-up of the day.

  - https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/brazil-paraguay-round-32-review-highlights

  Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/netherlands-morocco-review-highlights

**30 June**

- **Mexico 2-0 Ecuador** — Mexico moved on in front of home support. FIFA’s dedicated match-report URL and FIFA’s knockout fixture page list the result.

  Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/argentina-cabo-verde-match-report-highlights
- **Colombia 1-0 Ghana** — Colombia sealed the final remaining Round-of-16 berth.  
  Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/colombia-ghana-match-report-highlights

## Notable results and upsets

### Biggest shocks
1. **Paraguay knocked out Germany**  
   Germany, four-time champions, exited after a **1-1 draw and 4-3 penalty defeat**. FIFA’s preview for Paraguay-France calls it a “**stunning elimination of Germany**.”  
   Sources:  
   - https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/paraguay-france-live-stream-team-news-tickets  
   - https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/brazil-paraguay-round-32-review-highlights

2. **Morocco eliminated the Netherlands**  
   Morocco beat the Dutch **3-2 on penalties after a 1-1 draw**, another headline upset and a continuation of Morocco’s big-tournament threat after their 2022 semi-final run.  
   Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/netherlands-morocco-review-highlights

### Biggest escapes
1. **Belgium 3-2 Senegal (AET)**  
   Belgium looked out before staging a dramatic comeback from **2-0 down**.  
   Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/belgium-senegal-review-highlights

2. **Argentina 3-2 Cabo Verde (AET)**  
   The defending champions were pushed to extra time by tournament surprise package Cabo Verde.  
   Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/argentina-cabo-verde-match-report-highlights

3. **Brazil 2-1 Japan**  
   Brazil needed a late Martinelli goal rather than cruising through.  
   Source: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/brazil-japan-review-highlights

- **Canada**
- **Brazil**
- **Paraguay**
- **Morocco**
- **France**
- **Mexico**
- **Norway**
- **England**
- **Belgium**

- https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket  
- https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/scores-fixtures

## Upcoming fixtures

As of today, the tournament is entering the **Round of 16**. FIFA’s fixtures and match previews list the following ties:

# Recap: Super Bowl LX and the 2026 NBA Finals

The two marquee U.S. championships of the 2025–26 sports season delivered very different but equally historic outcomes. In the NFL, the **Seattle Seahawks** won a defense-driven **29-13** Super Bowl over the **New England Patriots**. In the NBA, the **New York Knicks** beat the **San Antonio Spurs** in **five games**, ending one of the longest championship droughts in major American team sports.

---

## Super Bowl LX

**Matchup:** Seattle Seahawks vs. New England Patriots  
**Date:** **Sunday, Feb. 8, 2026**  
**Venue:** **Levi’s Stadium, Santa Clara, California**  
**Final score:** **Seahawks 29, Patriots 13**  
**Super Bowl MVP:** **Kenneth Walker III, RB, Seahawks**

### Who played and what happened
Seattle beat New England **29-13** in **Super Bowl LX** on Feb. 8, 2026, at Levi’s Stadium. NFL.com’s recap and AP’s game report both describe the Seahawks’ win as a defense-led performance; Seattle built a **19-0 lead** before New England got back into the scoring column in the fourth quarter.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx-what-we-learned-from-seattle-s-29-13-win  
- https://apnews.com/article/super-bowl-seahawks-patriots-24ad67503a342a7e24348e66986250ab  

Seattle’s defense was the story. FOX Sports’ live game coverage reported **six sacks** and **three takeaways**, including a defensive touchdown, while AP similarly emphasized the Seahawks’ control on that side of the ball.  
Sources:  
- https://www.foxsports.com/live-blog/nfl/super-bowl-lx-live-updates-score-seahawks-patriots  
- https://apnews.com/article/super-bowl-seahawks-patriots-24ad67503a342a7e24348e66986250ab  

### MVP
**Kenneth Walker III** was named **Super Bowl LX MVP**. NFL.com reported that Walker rushed for **135 yards on 27 carries**, added **2 catches for 26 yards**, and finished with **161 total yards from scrimmage**.  
Source:  
- https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-rb-kenneth-walker-iii-named-super-bowl-lx-mvp  

### Why it was historically significant
This win gave the Seahawks their **second Super Bowl title**. NFL.com and AP both frame it as the franchise’s second Lombardi Trophy.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nfl.com/news/social-media-reacts-to-seahawks-win-over-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx  
- https://apnews.com/article/seattle-seahawks-super-bowl-champs-afda3b015712f8f16bb78adf8f0fe71b  

Walker’s MVP also carried a clear historical marker: AP reported that he became the **first running back in 28 years** to win Super Bowl MVP. NFL.com added that his **135 rushing yards were the most in a Super Bowl since Terrell Davis ran for 157**.  
Sources:  
- https://apnews.com/article/super-bowl-mvp-walker-07af8f82476cde717e5711762d307040  
- https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-rb-kenneth-walker-iii-named-super-bowl-lx-mvp  

---

## 2026 NBA Finals

**Matchup:** New York Knicks vs. San Antonio Spurs  
**Series dates:** **June 3–13, 2026**  
**Series result:** **Knicks won 4-1**  
**Clinching game:** **Game 5, Saturday, June 13, 2026**  
**Game 5 final score:** **Knicks 94, Spurs 90**  
**NBA Finals MVP:** **Jalen Brunson, G, Knicks**

### Who played and what happened
The **Knicks** and **Spurs** met in the 2026 NBA Finals, and New York won the series **4-1**. NBA.com’s official playoff schedule lists the game-by-game results:

- **Game 1:** Knicks 105, Spurs 95  
- **Game 2:** Knicks 105, Spurs 104  
- **Game 3:** Spurs 115, Knicks 111  
- **Game 4:** Knicks 107, Spurs 106  
- **Game 5:** Knicks 94, Spurs 90  

Source:  
- https://www.nba.com/news/2026-nba-playoffs-schedule  

New York clinched the title in **Game 5 on June 13, 2026**, winning **94-90** in San Antonio. AP reported that **Jalen Brunson scored 45 points**, including **13 straight points in the fourth quarter**, to close out the series. NBA.com reported the same scoring total in its Finals MVP coverage and game summary.  
Sources:  
- https://apnews.com/article/nba-finals-game-5-spurs-knicks-372c259a94837166818ca7386e678852  
- https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp  
- https://www.nba.com/game/nyk-vs-sas-0042500405  

### MVP
**Jalen Brunson** won the **Bill Russell NBA Finals MVP** award. NBA.com’s official announcement says Brunson’s **45 points in Game 5** set a **Knicks Finals record** for a single game.  
Source:  
- https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp  

### Why it was historically significant
The biggest headline was simple: the Knicks won their **first NBA championship since 1973**, ending a **53-year title drought**. AP and NBA.com both note that it was New York’s **third NBA title** overall.  
Sources:  
- https://apnews.com/article/nba-finals-knicks-champions-0391290b598972abdf5dd230c2f49d82  
- https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp  

The series itself was historically unusual. NBA coverage notes that:

- The Knicks won the **first two games** of an NBA Finals for the **first time in franchise history**.  
- It was the **first Finals since 1995** in which a team won the **first two road games**.  
- It was the **first since 1993** in which the **road team won the first three games**.  

Source summary:  
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/2026_NBA_Finals  

The defining swing game was **Game 4**, when the Knicks came back from a **29-point deficit in the third quarter** to win **107-106** on an **OG Anunoby tip-in with 1.2 seconds left**. NBA.com referred to it as a **historic comeback**.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nba.com/news/live-updates-2026-nba-finals-media-day-game-5-knicks-spurs-talk-to-the-media-after-game-4s-historic-comeback  
- https://www.nba.com/watch/list/collection/2025-26-game-recaps  

---

## Takeaway for the sports desk

**Super Bowl LX** was a classic defense-and-ground-game championship: Seattle beat New England **29-13** on **Feb. 8, 2026**, and **Kenneth Walker III** won MVP after a **135-yard rushing performance**. Its historical weight came from the Seahawks winning their **second Super Bowl** and Walker becoming the **first running back in 28 years** to win the award.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx-what-we-learned-from-seattle-s-29-13-win  
- https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-rb-kenneth-walker-iii-named-super-bowl-lx-mvp  
- https://apnews.com/article/super-bowl-mvp-walker-07af8f82476cde717e5711762d307040  

**The 2026 NBA Finals** were a drought-ending New York championship story: the Knicks beat the Spurs **4-1**, clinching **94-90** in Game 5 on **June 13, 2026**, with **Jalen Brunson** scoring **45** and winning Finals MVP. The result was historic because it gave the Knicks their **first championship in 53 years**, punctuated by a **29-point comeback in Game 4** and a rare run of road success at the start of the series.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nba.com/news/2026-nba-playoffs-schedule  
- https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp  
- https://apnews.com/article/nba-finals-game-5-spurs-knicks-372c259a94837166818ca7386e678852  
- https://apnews.com/article/nba-finals-knicks-champions-0391290b598972abdf5dd230c2f49d82  

Short answer: **Super Bowl LX — Seahawks 29, Patriots 13; MVP Kenneth Walker III. 2026 NBA Finals — Knicks beat Spurs 4-1, clinching Game 5 94-90; Finals MVP Jalen Brunson.**

States the New York Knicks beat the San Antonio Spurs in five games and that it was their first NBA championship since 1973.

The report does not mention the Spurs beating the Thunder 4-3 in the West finals or the Knicks sweeping the Cavaliers 4-0 in the East.

gptr.report:
## Recap: Super Bowl LX and the 2026 NBA Finals

The two biggest U.S. championship events of the 2025-26 sports season both ended with long-awaited or franchise-defining results: the Seattle Seahawks won Super Bowl LX, and the New York Knicks won the 2026 NBA Finals. In both cases, the winners claimed their franchise’s second or third title respectively, and each championship carried clear historical weight beyond the final score.

### Super Bowl LX: Seahawks 29, Patriots 13  
**Date:** February 8, 2026  
**Matchup:** Seattle Seahawks vs. New England Patriots  
**Site:** Levi’s Stadium, Santa Clara, California  
**Final score:** Seahawks 29, Patriots 13  
**MVP:** Kenneth Walker III, Seattle RB ([NFL](https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-rb-kenneth-walker-iii-named-super-bowl-lx-mvp); [AP](https://apnews.com/article/super-bowl-mvp-walker-07af8f82476cde717e5711762d307040); [Pro Football Reference](https://www.pro-football-reference.com/super-bowl))

Seattle beat New England 29-13 in Super Bowl LX on Feb. 8, 2026, securing the **second Super Bowl title in Seahawks franchise history**. Official box scores show Seattle led **3-0 after the first quarter, 9-0 at halftime, 12-0 after three quarters, and then pulled away 29-13 in the fourth**. ([ESPN box score](https://www.espn.com/nfl/boxscore/_/gameId/401772988); [AP live recap](https://apnews.com/live/super-bowl-60-2026-updates))

Kenneth Walker III was named MVP after rushing for **135 yards on 27 carries** and adding **26 receiving yards**, giving him **161 yards from scrimmage**. NFL.com noted that his **135 rushing yards were the most in a Super Bowl since Terrell Davis ran for 157 in Super Bowl XXXII**. ([NFL](https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-rb-kenneth-walker-iii-named-super-bowl-lx-mvp))

What made the outcome historically significant was not just the championship total, but the way Seattle won it. The Seahawks’ defense forced **three turnovers**, including a **Uchenna Nwosu fumble-return touchdown**, and NFL analysis afterward described the performance as **one of the most impressive defensive efforts a team has produced in a Super Bowl**. ([AP game story](https://apnews.com/article/super-bowl-seahawks-patriots-24ad67503a342a7e24348e66986250ab); [NFL analysis](https://www.nfl.com/news/sam-darnold-silences-skeptics-as-seahawks-dominate-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx))

There were also two notable historical sidebars:

- The win gave Seattle its **second Lombardi Trophy**, the first since the 2013 season’s Super Bowl XLVIII title. ([AP](https://apnews.com/article/seattle-seahawks-super-bowl-champs-afda3b015712f8f16bb78adf8f0fe71b))
- Kicker **Jason Myers made five field goals**, which NFL.com called a **Super Bowl record**, and scored **17 of Seattle’s 29 points**. ([NFL](https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-jason-myers-first-200-point-scorer-record-five-field-goals-super-bowl-lx))

In short, Super Bowl LX was a defensive, field-position game that Seattle controlled for most of the night, with Walker’s volume and efficiency giving the Seahawks the offensive edge they needed.

---

### 2026 NBA Finals: Knicks 4, Spurs 1  
**Dates:** Series opened June 4, 2026, and ended June 13, 2026  
**Matchup:** New York Knicks vs. San Antonio Spurs  
**Series result:** Knicks won 4-1  
**Clinching game:** Knicks 94, Spurs 90 in Game 5  
**Finals MVP:** Jalen Brunson ([NBA Finals hub](https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/nba-finals); [NBA MVP story](https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp); [AP](https://apnews.com/article/knicks-nba-championship-b86c921cf7116980fe01ff4524cfaf48))

The Knicks beat the Spurs in **five games**, closing out the series with a **94-90 road win in San Antonio on June 13, 2026**. The NBA’s official game page and Finals notes confirm the series score at **4-1**. ([NBA game page](https://www.nba.com/game/nyk-vs-sas-0042500405); [NBA Finals notes](https://pr.nba.com/2026-nba-finals-game-notes))

Jalen Brunson was the clear star of the series and the clincher. In Game 5, he scored **45 points**, including **15 in the fourth quarter**, and NBA.com called it a **Knicks Finals record** for points in a game. He was then awarded the **Bill Russell NBA Finals MVP Trophy**. ([NBA MVP story](https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp); [NBA takeaways](https://www.nba.com/news/4-takeaways-knicks-spurs-game-5); [AP Game 5 recap](https://apnews.com/article/nba-finals-game-5-spurs-knicks-372c259a94837166818ca7386e678852))

The historical significance of the Knicks’ title was enormous:

- It was **New York’s first NBA championship since 1973**, ending a **53-year drought**. ([NBA live recap](https://www.nba.com/news/live-updates-2026-nba-finals-game-5-knicks-seek-first-title-since-1973); [AP](https://apnews.com/article/knicks-nba-championship-b86c921cf7116980fe01ff4524cfaf48))
- It was the **third NBA title in franchise history**. ([NBA Finals hub](https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/nba-finals); [Wikipedia summary, consistent but secondary](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/NBA_Finals))
- NBA.com also described the series as **historically clutch**, noting that New York **overcame double-digit deficits in all five games** and won four of them, while Brunson scored **22 clutch points in the series**, the most by any player in the Finals in the last 15 years of available comparison cited by the league. ([NBA historical analysis](https://www.nba.com/news/2026-nba-finals-prove-historically-clutch))
- Game 4 was especially historic: the Knicks rallied from **29 points down** to win **107-106** on OG Anunoby’s tip-in with **1.2 seconds left**, which NBA.com billed as the **largest comeback in NBA Finals history**. ([AP Game 4](https://apnews.com/article/nba-finals-spurs-knicks-game-4-ba83cdcb98f92d0c9fffd32a5745c97c); [NBA Starting 5](https://www.nba.com/news/starting-5-finals-game-5-tonight-will-there-be-a-champion-or-a-game-6))

That combination — ending a 53-year title drought, doing it with repeated comebacks, and getting a 45-point closeout from Brunson — made the 2026 Finals one of the most dramatic championship runs in Knicks history.

---

## Bottom line for the desk

- **Super Bowl LX:** Seattle Seahawks 29, New England Patriots 13, on **Feb. 8, 2026**. **Kenneth Walker III** won MVP after **161 scrimmage yards**, and Seattle captured its **second Super Bowl title**, powered by a dominant defense and a Super Bowl-record **five field goals by Jason Myers**. ([NFL](https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-rb-kenneth-walker-iii-named-super-bowl-lx-mvp), [AP](https://apnews.com/article/super-bowl-seahawks-patriots-24ad67503a342a7e24348e66986250ab), [ESPN](https://www.espn.com/nfl/boxscore/_/gameId/401772988))

- **2026 NBA Finals:** New York Knicks beat the San Antonio Spurs **4-1**, clinching with a **94-90** Game 5 win on **June 13, 2026**. **Jalen Brunson** was Finals MVP after a **45-point** closeout, and New York won its **first NBA title since 1973**, ending a **53-year drought**. ([NBA](https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp), [NBA Finals hub](https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/nba-finals), [AP](https://apnews.com/article/knicks-nba-championship-b86c921cf7116980fe01ff4524cfaf48))

Short answer: **Super Bowl LX was Seattle’s 29-13 win over New England on Feb. 8, 2026, with Kenneth Walker III as MVP; the 2026 NBA Finals were New York’s 4-1 win over San Antonio, capped by a 94-90 Game 5 on June 13, with Jalen Brunson as Finals MVP.**

The 98th Academy Awards wrapped on Sunday, March 15, 2026, at the Dolby Theatre at Ovation Hollywood in Los Angeles, with Conan O’Brien returning as host for a second straight year. The ceremony honored films released in 2025 and aired live on ABC, with streaming on Hulu. The Academy’s official ceremony page, Disney’s Oscars guide, and ABC News all confirm the date, venue, and host.  
Sources: https://www.oscars.org/oscars/ceremonies/2026 ; https://thewaltdisneycompany.com/news/oscars-2026-faq ; https://abcnews.com/GMA/Culture/oscars-2026-what-to-know/story?id=130270230

The night belonged chiefly to **“One Battle After Another,”** which won **six Oscars**, including **Best Picture**, **Best Director** for **Paul Thomas Anderson**, **Best Supporting Actor** for **Sean Penn**, **Best Adapted Screenplay**, **Best Film Editing**, and the inaugural **Best Casting** award. Reuters, ABC News, AP, and the Academy’s official winners page all match on that total and those key wins.  
Sources: https://www.oscars.org/oscars/ceremonies/2026 ; https://www.reuters.com/lifestyle/oscars-2026-winners-list-sinners-one-battle-after-another-sweep-2026-03-16 ; https://abcnews.com/culture/live-updates/oscars-2026?id=130977802 ; https://apnews.com/live/oscars-2026-updates

**“Sinners”** was the other major force of the evening, finishing with **four Oscars**: **Best Actor** for **Michael B. Jordan**, **Best Original Screenplay** for **Ryan Coogler**, **Best Original Score** for **Ludwig Göransson**, and **Best Cinematography** for **Autumn Durald Arkapaw**. Reuters reported that Arkapaw became the first woman and first Black cinematographer to win that category.  
Sources: https://www.oscars.org/oscars/ceremonies/2026 ; https://www.reuters.com/business/media-telecom/oscars-top-prize-up-grabs-unease-hangs-over-hollywood-2026-03-15 ; https://abcnews.com/GMA/Culture/michael-jordan-leonardo-dicaprio-embrace-after-jordans-oscar/story?id=131116360

records.prompt: Write a mission summary for a science desk on NASA's Artemis II mission. Cover when it launched and returned, the crew and the historic firsts they represent, what the mission accomplished, any records set, and what it means for the Artemis program going forward. Be specific about dates, names, and figures.

baseline.report:
**Mission summary: NASA’s Artemis II**

NASA’s Artemis II mission launched on **April 1, 2026**, aboard the **Space Launch System (SLS)** from **Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center, Florida**, and returned with a **Pacific splashdown off the coast of California on April 10, 2026, at 8:07 p.m. EDT**. NASA describes it as the **first crewed mission of the Artemis program** and the **first crewed lunar flyby in more than 50 years**. NASA’s postflight material describes the flight as a roughly **10-day** mission around the Moon and back.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/liftoff-nasa-launches-astronauts-on-historic-artemis-moon-mission  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-live-re-entry-updates  
- https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii  

## Crew and historic firsts

The four-person crew was:  
- **Reid Wiseman** (NASA), commander  
- **Victor Glover** (NASA), pilot  
- **Christina Koch** (NASA), mission specialist  
- **Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency), mission specialist  

NASA and CSA identified the crew as historically significant well before launch. **Christina Koch** became the **first woman to fly to the vicinity of the Moon** on Artemis II. **Victor Glover** became the **first person of color to take part in a lunar mission**. **Jeremy Hansen** became the **first Canadian** to fly on a lunar mission; CSA also describes him as the first Canadian to go on a mission around the Moon. NASA’s original crew announcement also framed Artemis II as the **first crew under Artemis**.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-names-astronauts-to-next-moon-mission-first-crew-under-artemis  
- https://www.asc-csa.gc.ca/eng/missions/artemis-ii  
- https://www.asc-csa.gc.ca/eng/missions/artemis-ii/mission.asp  

## What the mission accomplished

Artemis II was fundamentally a **crewed test flight in lunar space** intended to verify that **Orion, SLS, ground systems, mission operations, and recovery procedures** work as required before NASA attempts a lunar landing mission. NASA’s official **Artemis II Priorities and Objectives** document says the mission’s primary goal was to demonstrate the ability of systems and teams to **sustain a crew in the flight environment and return them safely to Earth**, while also validating key operations such as crew rescue, Earth-departure configuration, service module propulsion upgrades, life-support performance, and post-splashdown recovery.  
Source:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/03/artemis-ii-mission-prioirities-objectives-.pdf?emrc=9fdebb  

During the flight, the crew completed a lunar flyby and operated Orion in deep space, testing the spacecraft in the environment it was designed for. NASA said the mission’s goals included confirming the systems needed to support astronauts in deep-space exploration and preparing for a sustained human presence at the Moon. NASA also emphasized that Artemis II demonstrated **life-support systems with crew aboard for the first time** on an Artemis mission and laid the groundwork for later lunar surface operations.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/liftoff-nasa-launches-astronauts-on-historic-artemis-moon-mission  
- https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii  

## Records and milestones

Artemis II set a new **human-spaceflight distance record**. On **April 6, 2026**, NASA announced that the crew had surpassed the previous mark set by **Apollo 13 in 1970**. NASA said Orion reached a **maximum distance from Earth of 252,756 miles**, establishing a new record for the farthest humans have traveled from Earth.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates  

NASA also highlighted a series of visual and operational milestones during the lunar flyby, including crew observations of **Earthrise**, **Earthset**, and a period of communications blackout while Orion passed behind the Moon, all of which helped validate navigation, communications, and mission operations in cislunar space.  
Source:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates  

## What Artemis II means for the Artemis program

Artemis II is the program’s critical **bridge from uncrewed testing to crewed lunar operations**. Artemis I proved the integrated SLS-Orion-ground system without astronauts; Artemis II tested that architecture **with people on board** in deep space. NASA says the mission was designed to **confirm spacecraft systems and operational procedures before future missions**, especially **Artemis III**, the mission intended to return astronauts to the lunar surface. NASA continues to describe Artemis as the framework through which it plans to establish a **long-term presence at the Moon for science and exploration** and to use lunar missions as preparation for **future human missions to Mars**.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-names-astronauts-to-next-moon-mission-first-crew-under-artemis  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/liftoff-nasa-launches-astronauts-on-historic-artemis-moon-mission  

For a science desk, the key editorial takeaway is that Artemis II was not a landing mission but a **high-stakes systems validation flight**: it proved NASA could send a crew safely beyond low Earth orbit again, operate around the Moon, and bring astronauts home after a deep-space mission. Its crew also embodied the broadened international and demographic scope NASA wants Artemis to represent. That combination—technical validation, operational rehearsal, and symbolic firsts—makes Artemis II the mission that moved Artemis from ambition to demonstrated human capability.

**Short answer:** Artemis II launched **April 1, 2026** and splashed down **April 10, 2026**; its crew—**Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch, and Jeremy Hansen**—included the **first woman**, **first person of color**, and **first Canadian** on a lunar mission, tested Orion/SLS in deep space, set a new **farthest-distance-from-Earth human spaceflight record of 252,756 miles**, and cleared a major path toward **Artemis III** and later lunar missions.
