gptr.report:
# NASA Artemis II mission summary

## Overview

Artemis II was NASA’s first crewed Artemis mission and the first crewed flight of the Space Launch System (SLS) rocket and Orion spacecraft. It sent four astronauts on an approximately 10-day lunar flyby mission to test Orion’s systems with humans aboard in deep space, a step NASA says is meant to “lay the groundwork for future crewed Artemis missions.” NASA launched Artemis II from Kennedy Space Center on **April 1, 2026**, and Orion splashed down in the Pacific on **April 10, 2026**.  
Sources: NASA mission overview: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; NASA launch coverage: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates ; NASA return coverage: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-re-entry-live-updates ; NASA mission FAQ: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions ; NASA media advisory: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/artemis-2/nasa-sets-coverage-for-artemis-ii-moon-mission

## Launch and return

NASA says Artemis II lifted off from **Launch Complex 39B at Kennedy Space Center in Florida at 6:35 p.m. EDT on Wednesday, April 1, 2026**. The mission ended when Orion **landed in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of California at 8:07 p.m. EDT on Friday, April 10, 2026**. During the flight, NASA consistently described the mission as an **approximately 10-day** journey around the Moon and back.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates ; https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-re-entry-live-updates ; https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions

## Crew and the historic firsts

The Artemis II crew was:

- **Reid Wiseman** (NASA) — commander  
- **Victor Glover** (NASA) — pilot  
- **Christina Koch** (NASA) — mission specialist  
- **Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency) — mission specialist  

NASA’s crew materials and widely cited institutional summaries identify the mission’s historic firsts as:

- **Christina Koch** became the **first woman to go to the Moon** on a lunar mission.  
- **Victor Glover** became the **first person of color to go to the Moon** on a lunar mission.  
- **Jeremy Hansen** became the **first Canadian to go to the Moon**.  

NASA’s Artemis II crew page confirms the crew assignments; a Smithsonian Air and Space summary of NASA’s announcement clearly states the firsts above.  
Sources: NASA mission page: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; NASA crew feature: https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew/ ; NASA special Artemis II page: https://www3.nasa.gov/specials/artemis-ii ; Smithsonian National Air and Space Museum: https://airandspace.si.edu/stories/editorial/meet-crew-artemis-ii

## What the mission accomplished

### 1) It completed the first crewed test of SLS and Orion
NASA describes Artemis II as the **first mission with crew aboard** SLS and Orion. The central purpose was to confirm that Orion’s systems work as designed with astronauts aboard in the actual deep-space environment.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; https://www3.nasa.gov/specials/artemis-ii ; https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/artemis-2/nasa-sets-coverage-for-artemis-ii-moon-mission

### 2) It tested life-support and habitability systems with astronauts aboard
NASA stated before launch that Artemis II would test **Orion’s life-support systems for the first time with humans aboard**. During the mission, the crew conducted operations and evaluations tied to the spacecraft’s deep-space habitability and safety systems, including suit demonstrations and other crew procedures.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/artemis-2/nasa-sets-coverage-for-artemis-ii-moon-mission ; https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/05/artemis-ii-flight-day-5-crew-demos-suits-readies-for-lunar-flyby ; https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions

### 3) It flew a lunar flyby profile that validated mission operations beyond low Earth orbit
NASA says Artemis II sent astronauts **beyond low Earth orbit for the first time in more than 50 years**, executing a lunar flyby and returning safely to Earth. NASA’s visualization page says Orion flew by the lunar far side at an altitude of roughly **3,000 to 9,000 miles** above the Moon, depending on trajectory segment, as part of the planned flyby profile.  
Sources: NASA SVS mission preview: https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/14934 ; NASA SVS trajectory page: https://svs.gsfc.nasa.gov/20412

### 4) It gathered operational and engineering data for future missions
After splashdown, NASA said engineers immediately began detailed assessments of how Orion, SLS, and ground systems performed. NASA specifically highlighted post-flight analysis of **key systems and subsystems** as part of preparing for future flights. NASA also noted that Orion’s **optical navigation camera** collected imagery of Earth and the Moon at different phases and distances to help certify the system for future crewed missions.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments ; https://www.nasa.gov/stem-content/artemis-ii-observe-the-moon

## Records and milestones

### Farthest human spaceflight distance from Earth
NASA announced on **April 6, 2026** that the Artemis II crew **surpassed the Apollo 13 record** for the farthest distance humans had traveled from Earth. NASA’s mission blog says Orion reached a maximum distance of **252,756 miles from Earth**, setting a new human-spaceflight record.  
Sources: NASA news release: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight ; NASA mission blog: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/06/artemis-ii-flight-day-6-lunar-flyby-updates

### First crewed deep-space mission of the Artemis era
NASA characterizes Artemis II as the **first crewed Artemis mission** and the first time astronauts traveled beyond low Earth orbit in over half a century under the new lunar campaign. That makes it a programmatic milestone even apart from the distance record.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/artemis-2/nasa-sets-coverage-for-artemis-ii-moon-mission ; https://www3.nasa.gov/specials/artemis-ii

### Media milestone
NASA also said Artemis II **broke the agency’s streaming record**, an indication of the mission’s unusually broad public reach.  
Source: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-crew-sets-for-final-burn-splashdown

## Why Artemis II matters for the Artemis program

NASA’s own framing is clear: Artemis II was the proving mission between the uncrewed Artemis I test flight and the planned return of astronauts to the lunar surface on Artemis III. By flying crew on Orion in deep space, testing life-support and spacecraft operations, validating navigation and reentry performance, and collecting post-flight engineering data, Artemis II reduced risk for later missions.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii ; https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions ; https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-on-track-for-future-missions-with-initial-artemis-ii-assessments

NASA’s Artemis III page says the next step is a landing mission at the lunar south pole using Orion and a human landing system, while Gateway is to support later missions. A separate NASA news release says Artemis III “**builds on the successful Artemis II flight completed in April**,” directly linking Artemis II’s results to the next crewed lunar campaign phase.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/artemis-iii ; https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-marches-toward-artemis-iii-mission-in-2027-names-crew-members

## Bottom line for a science desk

Artemis II was not a landing mission; it was a **crewed systems-validation mission**. But it was a consequential one. NASA launched the mission on **April 1, 2026**, returned the crew safely on **April 10, 2026**, flew the first astronauts of the Artemis era around the Moon, set a **new human-distance-from-Earth record of 252,756 miles**, and marked major representation milestones with **Christina Koch, Victor Glover, and Jeremy Hansen**. Most importantly, NASA treats Artemis II as the mission that demonstrated Orion and SLS with crew in deep space and cleared the path toward **Artemis III** and later lunar-surface operations.  
Sources: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates ; https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-re-entry-live-updates ; https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight ; https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-marches-toward-artemis-iii-mission-in-2027-names-crew-members

Short answer: Artemis II launched on April 1, 2026 and returned on April 10, 2026; its four-person crew set historic firsts for a woman, a person of color, and a Canadian on a lunar mission, tested Orion and SLS with humans in deep space, set a new farthest-human-spaceflight record at 252,756 miles from Earth, and paved the way for Artemis III.
